title: Battle of Peshawar (1001)

infobox.value: This article **relies excessively on [references](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability") to [primary sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")**. Please improve this article by adding [secondary or tertiary sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research").  *Find sources:* ["Battle of Peshawar" 1001](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Battle+of+Peshawar%22+1001) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Battle+of+Peshawar%22+1001+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Battle+of+Peshawar%22+1001&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Battle+of+Peshawar%22+1001+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Battle+of+Peshawar%22+1001) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Battle+of+Peshawar%22+1001&acc=on&wc=on) *(February 2026)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))*

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Battle+of+Peshawar%22+1001&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Battle+of+Peshawar%22+1001+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Battle+of+Peshawar%22+1001) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Battle+of+Peshawar%22+1001&acc=on&wc=on) *(February 2026)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

was fought on 27 November 1001 between the [Ghaznavid](/wiki/Ghaznavid "Ghaznavid") army of [Mahmud of Ghazni](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghazni "Mahmud of Ghazni") and the [Hindu Shahi](/wiki/Hindu_Shahi "Hindu Shahi") army of [Jayapala](/wiki/Jayapala "Jayapala"), near [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"). Jayapala was defeated and captured, and as a result of the humiliation of the defeat, he later [immolated](/wiki/Self-immolation "Self-immolation") himself in a funeral pyre. This is the first of many major battles in the expansion of the [Ghaznavid Empire](/wiki/Ghaznavids "Ghaznavids") into the Indian subcontinent by Mahmud.

[Ziad 2016](#CITEREFZiad2016), pp. 627–628 "Mahmud's second campaign into Hindustan, from September 1001 to April 1002, resulted in the annexation of Peshawar and Udabhandapura (the eastern capital of the Hindu Shahis on the Indus River, near present-day Attock). The Hindu Shahis quickly abandoned their capitals, and Gandhara was brought into the Ghaznavid fold."

Susan Wise Bauer (2010). [*The History of the Medieval World: From the Conversion of Constantine to the First Crusade*](https://books.google.com/books?id=1u2oP2RihIgC&pg=PA554). W. W. Norton & Company. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-393-05975-5](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-393-05975-5 "Special:BookSources/978-0-393-05975-5").

["AMEER NASIR-OOD-DEEN SUBOOKTUGEEN"](https://web.archive.org/web/20160305021544/http://persian.packhum.org/persian/main?url=pf?file=06901021&ct=12). *persian.packhum.org*. 5 March 2016. Archived from [the original](http://persian.packhum.org/persian/main?url=pf?file=06901021&ct=12) on 5 March 2016. Retrieved 9 April 2023.

Sir H. M. Elliot (1869). ["Chapter II, Tarikh Yamini or Kitabu-l Yamini by Al Utbi"](https://archive.org/stream/cu31924073036729#page/n33/mode/2up). *[The History of India, as Told by Its Own Historians. The Muhammadan Period](/wiki/The_History_of_India,_as_Told_by_Its_Own_Historians._The_Muhammadan_Period "The History of India, as Told by Its Own Historians. The Muhammadan Period")*. Trubner and Co. pp. 18–24.

Pradeep Barua (2006). [*The State at War in South Asia*](https://books.google.com/books?id=FIIQhuAOGaIC&pg=PA25). University of Nebraska Press. p. 25. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-8032-1344-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-8032-1344-1 "Special:BookSources/0-8032-1344-1").

1001 battle in the Ghaznavid campaigns in India

For other uses of "Battle of Peshawar", see [Battle of Peshawar (disambiguation)](/wiki/Battle_of_Peshawar_(disambiguation) "Battle of Peshawar (disambiguation)").

The **Battle of Peshawar** was fought on 27 November 1001 between the [Ghaznavid](/wiki/Ghaznavid "Ghaznavid") army of [Mahmud of Ghazni](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghazni "Mahmud of Ghazni") and the [Hindu Shahi](/wiki/Hindu_Shahi "Hindu Shahi") army of [Jayapala](/wiki/Jayapala "Jayapala"), near [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"). Jayapala was defeated and captured, and as a result of the humiliation of the defeat, he later [immolated](/wiki/Self-immolation "Self-immolation") himself in a funeral pyre. This is the first of many major battles in the expansion of the [Ghaznavid Empire](/wiki/Ghaznavids "Ghaznavids") into the Indian subcontinent by Mahmud.

## Background

In 962, [Alp-Tegin](/wiki/Alp-Tegin "Alp-Tegin"), a [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_peoples "Turkic peoples") [ghulam](/wiki/Ghilman "Ghilman") or slave soldier, who rose to be the commander of the army in [Khorasan](/wiki/Greater_Khorasan "Greater Khorasan") in the service of the [Samanids](/wiki/Samanids "Samanids"), seized [Ghazna](/wiki/Ghazna "Ghazna") and set himself up as a ruler there. In 997, Mahmud ascended the throne at Ghazni, a successor to [Sabuktigin](/wiki/Sabuktigin "Sabuktigin"), Mahmud started to vigorously expand his domain, and vowed to invade India every year until the northern lands were his.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The Hindu Shahi kingdom extended from Laghman to Kashmir and from Sirhind to Multan.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-satish-4) Hindu Shahi ruler Jayapala attacked the Ghaznavids, but was defeated, then again later when his army of a reported size of over 100,000 was beaten too.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Battle

In September 1001, Mahmud arrived at [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar") with a select group of 15,000 cavalry, and a large corps of *[ghazis](/wiki/Ghazis "Ghazis")* and [Afghans](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns").[[4]](#cite_note-satish-4) Jayapal advanced to confront Mahmud with an army of 12,000 cavalry, 30,000 infantry, and 100 war elephants, positioning himself directly in front of Mahmud's camp. The two armies clashed on 27 November 1001 CE. The battle continued until noon, when the Hindu forces, unable to withstand the Ghaznavids, broke and fled.[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6)

An account of the battle between the invading Turkic Ghaznavids and the Shahi kingdom was given by Al-Utbi in*[Tarikh Yamini](/wiki/Tarikh_Yamini "Tarikh Yamini")*.[[7]](#cite_note-7) According to Al-Utbi, Mahmud pitched his tent outside the city upon reaching Peshawar. Jayapala avoided action for some time waiting for reinforcements, and Mahmud then took the decision to attack with swords, arrows, and spears. Jayapala moved his cavalry and elephants to engage his opponent, but his army was decisively defeated.[[8]](#cite_note-history_of_india-8)[*[non-primary source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")*]

Jayapala, along with his fifteen sons and grandsons were captured, and valuable personal adornments were taken off the prisoners, including fifteen necklaces each valued at 80,000 dinars.[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6)

The number of Hindu dead ranged from 5,000[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6) to 15,000,[[8]](#cite_note-history_of_india-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[*[non-primary source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")*] and five hundred thousands were said to have been taken captive. Judging from the personal adornments taken off captured Hindus, Jayapala's army was not prepared for battle and thousands were taken captive as well.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Aftermath

### Battle of Hund

After the victory Mahmud proceeded to [Hund](/wiki/Hund,_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Hund, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") the capital of the Hindu Shahi dynasty. He launched a powerful assault and captured the town. The demoralized Shahi forces fled toward the mountain passes but were pursued and scattered with heavy slaughter. Ghaznavid cavalry cleared remaining resistance in the plains. Within weeks, the entire lower [Kabul valley](/wiki/Kabul_Valley "Kabul Valley") and surrounding region submitted to Mahmud.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

He returned to Ghazni in April 1002 after subjugating adjoining territories. As part of the peace terms Jayapal agreed to pay 250,000 dinars and deliver 50 elephants. He was released to return to his kingdom, but one son and one grandson were held as hostages until the conditions were met.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

There are differing views regarding the fate of Jayapala.

Historian [Henry Miers Elliot](/wiki/Henry_Miers_Elliot "Henry Miers Elliot"), who worked with the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company"), writes:

> Jayapala was bound and paraded, and a large ransom was paid for the release of members of his family. Jayapala felt the defeat to be a great humiliation, and later he built himself a funeral pyre, lit it, and threw himself into the fire.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

However, [Satish Chandra](/wiki/Satish_Chandra_(historian) "Satish Chandra (historian)") says:

> According to some later accounts, Jayapala had entered a funeral pyre following his defeat because he felt he had disgraced himself. The story that he had been taken prisoner by Mahmud and then released seems doubtful.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Mahmud later conquered the upper Indus region, and then in 1009, defeated Jayapala's son [Anandapala](/wiki/Anandapala "Anandapala") in a [battle at Chach](/wiki/Battle_of_Chach "Battle of Chach"). He then captured [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore") and [Multan](/wiki/Multan "Multan"), giving him control of the [Punjab region](/wiki/Punjab_region "Punjab region").[[4]](#cite_note-satish-4)

## See also

* [Janjua](/wiki/Janjua "Janjua")
* [History of Peshawar](/wiki/History_of_Peshawar "History of Peshawar")
* [History of India](/wiki/History_of_India "History of India")

## References

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** [Ziad 2016](#CITEREFZiad2016), pp. 627–628 "Mahmud's second campaign into Hindustan, from September 1001 to April 1002, resulted in the annexation of Peshawar and Udabhandapura (the eastern capital of the Hindu Shahis on the Indus River, near present-day Attock). The Hindu Shahis quickly abandoned their capitals, and Gandhara was brought into the Ghaznavid fold."
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** Susan Wise Bauer (2010). [*The History of the Medieval World: From the Conversion of Constantine to the First Crusade*](https://books.google.com/books?id=1u2oP2RihIgC&pg=PA554). W. W. Norton & Company. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-393-05975-5](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-393-05975-5 "Special:BookSources/978-0-393-05975-5").

[ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-8032-1344-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-8032-1344-1 "Special:BookSources/0-8032-1344-1").

Trubner and Co. pp. 24–26. "Swords flashed like lightning amid the blackness of clouds, and fountains of blood flowed like the fall of setting stars. The friends of God defeated their obstinate opponents, and quickly put them to a complete rout. Noon had not arrived when the Musulmans had wreaked their vengeance on the infidel enemies of God, killing 15,000 of them, spreading them like a carpet over the ground, and making them food for beasts and birds of prey. Fifteen elephants fell on the field of battle, as their legs, being pierced with arrows, became as motion-less as if they had been in a quagmire, and their trunks were cut with the swords of the valiant heroes."

"Sultan Mahmud behaved bravely and victory fell to him, he became famous as a Ghazi; and he captured Jaipal with fifteen men, who were some his sons and some his relations, and he killed five thousand Hindus and brought back much plunder."
